Commercial Mar 22, 2026

The Real Reason Why The Queen Of The Skies Wasn't A Perfect Fit For American Airlines

Why American Airlines turned its back on the double-decker jumbo jet.

American Airlines rejected the 747 due to operational constraints and route economics that favored smaller, more efficient wide-body aircraft.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 22, 2026

This Is Why Boeing Stopped Producing The 757

The iconic aircraft was once a workhorse of many fleets around the world.

Boeing's 757 production halt reflects evolving market demands and the economics of maintaining legacy aircraft programs against newer, more efficient competitors.

Commercial Mar 22, 2026

The Most Superior Aircraft To Replace The Airbus A380 On High-Capacity Routes

What can replace the superjumbo?

Airlines seeking A380 replacements must balance capacity needs against fuel efficiency and operational economics of modern widebody alternatives.

Commercial Mar 22, 2026

United Airlines To Debut New Polaris Suites Without Full Passenger Privacy

United’s new Polaris suites debut on 787-8s with locked doors due to certification delays, echoing a similar issue faced by American Airlines.

United's Polaris suite rollout on 787-8s lacks full privacy doors due to certification delays, mirroring American's earlier struggle with regulatory compliance.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 22, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective safety risk management requires organizations to systematically develop and implement controls while understanding that some residual risk will remain acceptable after mitigation efforts.

How Much Does A Boeing 777X Engine Cost?

The largest and most powerful commercial engine ever built, the GE9X is by no means cheap

The GE9X engine for the Boeing 777X represents a significant capital investment, with costs reflecting advanced technology and limited production volumes affecting aircraft economics.

Safety Mar 22, 2026

United Airlines Pilots Mistook Runway Centerline At Chicago O’Hare, Damaging Edge Lights

A United Airlines Boeing 737-700 Causes Damage To Runway Lights At ORD After Reportedly Incorrectly Lining Up With Runway Incorrectly.

Runway alignment errors remain a critical safety concern even for experienced crews, highlighting the need for enhanced visual guidance systems and procedural reinforcement.

Delta Air Lines Passenger Yells Racial Slur At Flight Attendant, Faces Up To 20 Years In Prison

Delta passenger may face prison sentence after allegedly using a racial slur against a flight attendant, highlighting ongoing unruly behavior risks.

Rising federal penalties for onboard misconduct underscore airlines' need for enhanced crew protection protocols and zero-tolerance enforcement.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 22, 2026

Here’s Why Southwest Airlines Can’t Fly All Of Its Boeing 737 MAX 8s Over The Pacific

These jets are subject to certain operational requirements.

Southwest's 737 MAX 8 fleet faces ETOPS certification limitations that restrict transpacific operations, requiring Extended Twin-Engine Operations approval before expanding international routes.

Military Mar 22, 2026

The Tomcat’s Six Pack: The F-14 Six on Six Phoenix Test

Designed for fleet air defense, the F-14 was armed with a potent long-range missile and, with a powerful new radar, could engage multiple airborne targets at once. The United States Navy’s F-14 Tomcat was the most potent fleet defense aircraft of its time. Its AIM-54 Phoenix missile had a long ra...

The F-14 Tomcat's six simultaneous Phoenix missile engagements demonstrated revolutionary multi-target capability that defined naval air defense doctrine for decades.

See The Aviationist →

Military Mar 22, 2026

U.S. Army Receives DARPA’s Optionally Piloted H-60Mx Black Hawk

The U.S. Army will put the H-60Mx and Sikorsky’s MATRIX flight autonomy software through a rigorous operational testing campaign with real world logistics in contested scenarios. The U.S. Army received on Mar. 19, 2026, the H-60Mx Black Hawk Optionally Piloted Vehicle (OPV) at Fort Eustis, Virgin...

DARPA's optionally piloted H-60Mx Black Hawk advances military helicopter autonomy, enabling safer logistics operations in contested environments with reduced crew requirements.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 22, 2026

SpaceX offers details on orbital data center satellites

SpaceX Chief Executive Elon Musk revealed more technical, but not financial, details about his company’s plans to deploy an orbital data center constellation. The post SpaceX offers details on orbital data center satellites appeared first on SpaceNews.

SpaceX's orbital data center constellation represents a new commercial spaceflight application that could reshape satellite operations and in-orbit infrastructure economics.

Industry Mar 22, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Integrating advanced technologies across airline, airport, and flight operations can simultaneously expand capacity and reduce environmental impact as air traffic grows.

Commercial Mar 22, 2026

How Quvia helps airlines throw out the IFC playbook and take control

The inflight connectivity marketplace is moving very quickly. And aviation industry stakeholders have never seen anything like it before. The cycles are getting faster. The technology is evolving rapidly, including the antenna hardware. At least five or more broadband Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satell...

Airlines can now bypass traditional IFC vendors through marketplace platforms, gaining direct control over connectivity technology selection and reducing dependency on legacy providers.

Video Shows Saab 340 Airborne Radar Flying in Ukraine

The Saab 340 AEW, also called the S 100D Argus, will enhance Ukraine’s situational awareness when facing Russian cruise missile and kamikaze drone barrages. Ukraine appears to have received its first Airborne Early Warning and Control (AEW&C) aircraft, after the Swedish Saab 340 airborne radar wa...

Ukraine's acquisition of the Saab 340 AEW aircraft significantly enhances its air defense capability against Russian missile and drone threats through advanced airborne radar surveillance.

Military Mar 21, 2026

Iran Fires Two Intermediate-Range Ballistic Missiles at Diego Garcia Base

The attempted strike on Diego Garcia would mark a major escalation, highlighting Tehran’s apparent ability to target the strategic U.S.-UK base deep in the Indian Ocean and potentially put parts of Europe within reach. Iran has launched two missiles at the joint UK-U.S. base at Diego Garcia in th...

Strategic ballistic missile threats to Diego Garcia underscore critical vulnerabilities in remote U.S. military aviation infrastructure and regional air defense requirements.

Aerospace Mar 20, 2026

NASA’s X-59 Experimental Supersonic Aircraft Makes Second Flight

NASA’s quiet supersonic X-59 aircraft made its second flight on Friday, kicking off a series of dozens of test flights in 2026. Although the flight duration was abbreviated due to a technical issue, the team was able to collect information that will inform future tests. “Despite the early landing...

NASA's X-59 quiet supersonic technology demonstrates progress toward enabling commercial overland supersonic flight by reducing sonic boom disruption.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 20, 2026

NASA X-59 Second Flight Ends Early After In-Flight Warning

Test campaign set to continue soon despite in-flight issues.

NASA's X-59 experimental aircraft experienced mid-flight anomalies requiring early termination, but the quiet supersonic demonstrator program remains on track for continued testing essential to validating low-boom aviation technology.
